concepts.finding: Customer needs are abstract context-dependent statements describing benefits customers seek, distinct from product attributes.
concepts.implication: Search-intelligence must distinguish between surface-level query patterns and underlying user intents/JTBD.
concepts.open_question: How do we map search queries to abstract JTBD statements vs. product attributes?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Sentence-level intent labels outperform keyword-cluster labels for downstream product development decisions.

concepts.finding: Traditional VOC uses 20-30 qualitative experiential interviews producing ~100 abstract customer-need statements structured hierarchically.
concepts.implication: Ground-truth target: ~100 intent clusters per domain, structured hierarchically (3 levels).
concepts.open_question: What is the optimal granularity (number of clusters) for search intent taxonomies?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: A 3-level hierarchy provides the best balance of interpretability and coverage.

concepts.finding: 52% of UGC sentences contained at least one customer need; 9.2% contained two or more needs. 10% of most frequent needs appear in 54% of informative sentences.
concepts.implication: Search queries follow power-law distribution: few dominant intents, many rare ones.
concepts.open_question: How many rare intents must a search-intelligence product capture to provide value?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Capturing the top 10% of intent clusters covers >50% of search volume.

concepts.finding: Inter-coder accuracy was 70% (new analyst vs. three original analysts). Individual analysts identified 45-68% of universe needs.
concepts.implication: Human agreement on intent labeling is inherently ~70-80%. Use multiple coders.
concepts.open_question: What is acceptable inter-annotator agreement for search-intent taxonomies?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Majority-vote from 3 annotators achieves 80%+ precision on search intent labels.

concepts.finding: CNN achieves F1=74.0% for identifying informative sentences. LSTM-RNN: 73.4%. SVM: 65.7%. CNN with asymmetric costs: recall 85.3%, precision 65.2%.
concepts.implication: Deep learning classifiers outperform SVM by ~8-10 F1 points for informativeness detection.
concepts.open_question: What is the best architecture for identifying intent-bearing search queries?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: A BERT-based classifier achieves >80% F1 for informative query detection.

concepts.finding: CNN performance stabilizes after ~500 training sentences, with improvement up to 2,000+. Training under 1 minute on MacBook Pro for 1,000-4,000 samples.
concepts.implication: Labeling budgets can be modest: ~500-2,000 labeled examples.
concepts.open_question: What is the minimum labeled sample size for robust query informativeness classification?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: 500 labeled queries achieve >90% of the F1 achievable with 5,000 labels.

concepts.finding: CNN identified sentences containing 100% of frequent needs, 91% of rare needs, and 84% of new-to-holdout needs.
concepts.implication: Informativeness filters have different recall by frequency class. Frequency-stratified evaluation required.
concepts.open_question: Do intent classifiers show systematic bias against rare intents?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Intent classifiers achieve 90%+ recall on high-frequency intents but <70% on long-tail intents.

concepts.finding: Sentence embeddings (20-dim from word embedding averages) clustered with Ward's method separate primary needs in PCA.
concepts.implication: Simple averaged word embeddings + hierarchical clustering can separate high-level intent categories.
concepts.open_question: What embedding granularity best separates fine-grained search intents?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: BERT sentence embeddings achieve higher cluster purity than averaged Word2Vec by >15%.

concepts.finding: ML reduces manual review effort by 15-22%. At 200 sentences: 78% efficiency; at 400: 79%.
concepts.implication: ML pre-filtering reduces human labeling effort by 15-22%.
concepts.open_question: What is the cost savings of ML-assisted vs. fully manual taxonomy building?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: ML-filtered sampling identifies 20%+ more unique intents per dollar spent on annotation.

concepts.finding: Frequency of mention is marginally correlated with importance (ρ=0.21, p=0.06). Using only above-median frequency would miss 29% of high-importance needs.
concepts.implication: Frequency-based or popularity-based query clustering misses rare but strategically valuable intents.
concepts.open_question: How to distinguish rare-but-important intents from noise?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Embedding-based similarity to known high-value intents identifies rare important queries.

concepts.finding: Oral-care VOC hierarchy: 6 primary groups, 22 secondary groups, 86 tertiary needs.
concepts.implication: Search-intent taxonomy prototype can target 5-7 primary, 15-25 secondary categories.
concepts.open_question: What is a universal intent taxonomy schema that generalizes across categories?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: A 6-primary/22-secondary taxonomy fits most physical product categories.

concepts.finding: Word embeddings trained with skip-gram, k=5 window, d=20 dimensions. Sentence embeddings: average of word embeddings.
concepts.implication: Simple averaging of word embeddings is a strong, scalable baseline for intent clustering.
concepts.open_question: Does mean-pooling of BERT token embeddings outperform learned sentence embeddings for intent clustering?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Sentence-BERT embeddings improve NMI for intent clusters by 10+ points over averaged word embeddings.

concepts.finding: 6 query-reformulation states: New, Assistance, Content Change, Generalization, Reformulation, Specialization. Classified 964,780 sessions.
concepts.implication: Search behavior has 6 distinct reformulation types. Intent tracking should capture reformulation patterns.
concepts.open_question: Do query-reformulation states correspond to distinct user intents/JTBD?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Reformulation states correlate with shifts in JTBD categories.

concepts.finding: 71.64% of sessions contain only 1 query; 15.85% have 2 queries; 12.51% have 3+. Mean query length: 2.79 terms.
concepts.implication: Most sessions are single-query. Short queries dominate. Intent extraction must work with very short text.
concepts.open_question: How to extract reliable intents from single short queries (2-3 words)?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Query expansion via retrieved documents improves single-query intent classification by 20%+.

concepts.finding: Transition probability: from New state, 21% go to Reformulation, 22% to Specialization, 21% to Assistance. After Assistance, 58% go to Content Change.
concepts.implication: Users often reformulate or specialize immediately after initial query.
concepts.open_question: Can query-reformulation patterns predict intent shifts within a session?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: A sequence model over reformulation states predicts intent shifts with >70% accuracy.

concepts.finding: Misclassification analysis: 95.6% accuracy on 2,000 verified queries. Errors: misspelling (58.5%), cookie issues (25.8%), special characters (5.6%).
concepts.implication: Automated query classification feasible at >95% accuracy. Main errors: spelling normalization.
concepts.open_question: How robust are intent classifiers to query spelling variations?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Spell-normalization preprocessor improves intent classification accuracy by 3-5%.

concepts.finding: Second-order Markov model: precision 40%, coverage 13%. Best overall tradeoff.
concepts.implication: 2nd-order Markov best for reformulation prediction. Only last 2 states needed.
concepts.open_question: What is the optimal session context window for intent prediction?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: A transformer with context window of 3-5 queries outperforms 2nd-order Markov.

concepts.finding: Two core IR tasks: extracting features, developing relevance estimators. Evolution: BM25 → dense retrieval (BERT) → LLM-based retrieval.
concepts.implication: Intent clustering needs representation backbone + relevance/clustering estimator. Separable decisions.
concepts.open_question: Should representation and clustering share a model or be separate?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Separate representation and clustering models outperform end-to-end models.

concepts.finding: Modern IR follows 'retrieve-then-rerank' two-stage: bi-encoder selects top-k, cross-encoder refines.
concepts.implication: Two-stage clustering: fast bi-encoder clustering + cross-encoder refinement.
concepts.open_question: Is two-stage clustering more effective than single-stage?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Two-stage pipeline improves cluster quality by 15% over single-stage.

concepts.finding: Hard Negative Mining critical: In-Batch Negatives, Lexical Negatives (BM25), Iterative HNM (ANCE).
concepts.implication: Careful negative sampling improves embedding quality for distinguishing similar intents.
concepts.open_question: What negative sampling strategy best learns separable intent embeddings?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Combined IBN + lexical negatives produces 10% better intent separation than random negatives.

concepts.finding: Knowledge Distillation: student bi-encoder trained to mimic cross-encoder via KL divergence. Bridges effectiveness gap.
concepts.implication: Distilled bi-encoder can approach cross-encoder quality at fraction of cost.
concepts.open_question: Can distilled bi-encoder match cross-encoder quality for intent assignment?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Distilled bi-encoder achieves <5% quality gap with cross-encoder while 100x faster.

concepts.finding: Sparse retrieval (SPLADE): learned sparse vectors aligned to vocabulary, inverted-index compatible, CPU inference.
concepts.implication: Sparse intent representations offer interpretability and CPU-efficient deployment.
concepts.open_question: Are sparse or dense representations better for intent interpretability?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Sparse representations achieve higher human-interpretability scores for cluster labeling.

concepts.finding: Multi-vector models (ColBERT): token-level late interaction via MaxSim. Strong performance with efficient index.
concepts.implication: Multi-vector representations may capture multi-faceted intents better than single-vector.
concepts.open_question: Do multi-vector representations improve handling of multi-faceted queries?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: ColBERT-style multi-vector improves recall for multi-intent queries by 15%.

concepts.finding: LLM as retriever: scaling law - larger backbone LMs (7B+) improve bi-encoder retrieval. LLM2Vec enables bidirectional attention on decoder-only models.
concepts.implication: Larger embedding models outperform BERT-size. Decoder adaptation needed for Llama-family.
concepts.open_question: What is cost-benefit sweet spot for embedding model size?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: A 7B embedding model improves NMI by 10+ points over 350M BERT.

concepts.finding: LLM as reranker: pointwise (T5), pairwise, listwise (RankGPT). Zero-shot RankGPT-4 competitive without fine-tuning.
concepts.implication: LLMs can assign queries to intent clusters zero-shot. Useful for seed taxonomy creation.
concepts.open_question: Can prompted LLMs replace human annotation for intent taxonomy creation?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: GPT-4 zero-shot labeling achieves 80%+ agreement with expert annotators.

concepts.finding: Contrastive learning (InfoNCE) for bi-encoder training: maximizes similarity between query-positive document vs. negatives.
concepts.implication: Contrastive training is the standard for learning search-intent representations.
concepts.open_question: What constitutes a positive pair for intent representation learning?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Co-clicked documents as positive pairs produce better intent representations than same-session queries.

concepts.finding: Four fundamental problems: Measurement (experimentation often infeasible), Feedback (recommendations affect future behavior), Scale, Incentives.
concepts.implication: Search-intent system must account for feedback loops, measurement bias, and strategic manipulation.
concepts.open_question: How do feedback loops bias intent clusters?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Intent clusters from pre-recommendation logs differ systematically from post-recommendation logs.

concepts.finding: Offline metrics predict online performance across 6 simulated environments. Spearman rank correlations positive. But diminishing returns after a point.
concepts.implication: Offline metrics valid for screening methods, but chasing small improvements may not yield online gains.
concepts.open_question: What is the good enough threshold for offline cluster quality?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Beyond NMI=0.75, further improvements yield diminishing online gains.

concepts.finding: Feedback loops affect algorithms differently. TopPop improves over time. CF-NADE improves slower. AutoRec initially bad but quickly matches best.
concepts.implication: Some clustering algorithms more susceptible to feedback bias than others.
concepts.open_question: Which intent clustering methods are most/least susceptible to feedback-loop bias?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Frequency-based grouping exhibits stronger feedback amplification than embedding-based clustering.

concepts.finding: Higher embedding dimension (d=50 vs d=10) induces more bias in content targeting. More expressive algorithms incentivize more targeted content.
concepts.implication: Higher-dimensional embeddings may enable more biased or manipulative targeting.
concepts.open_question: What is the safe dimensionality that balances expressiveness and bias?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Intent embeddings with d<30 show 50% less demographic bias amplification than d>100.

concepts.finding: Pre-deployment audit of strategic creator incentives can identify misalignment before deployment.
concepts.implication: Simulate strategic responses before deploying an intent-clustering system.
concepts.open_question: What is a pre-deployment audit framework for search-intent systems?
concepts.testable_hypothesis: Pre-deployment simulation predicts 70%+ of real-world content manipulation patterns.
